favorites.dial: white-indigo guilloche, sensu PR, onyx crown cabochon
favorites.note: #1 want. Recent LoupeThis sold $3,520 (Mar) / $3,960 (Apr). Target sub-$2,500.

favorites.dial: German silver, hand-engraved Romans, 18k gold moon
favorites.note: Discontinued at 15pcs — successor is Type 3B-3 with lapis lazuli moon (5pc 2025-26).

favorites.dial: German silver dial, lapis lazuli night sky, 18k gold engraved moon
favorites.note: Type 3A successor. Just 5 pieces total 2025-2026. Apply via 2026 lottery.

favorites.dial: German silver, applied Roman numerals
favorites.note: Dress-watch flagship. 36mm hits the sweet spot. Secondary appears occasionally at Mr Watchley.

favorites.dial: German silver, alternative numeral configuration
favorites.note: Originally Armoury HK collaboration. Occasionally surfaces on secondary.

favorites.dial: rectangular dress, custom acrylic glass
favorites.note: Small rectangular tank. Below your round-case sweet spot but a deep-cut indie play.

favorites.dial: brown cinnamon urushi lacquer
favorites.note: Email info@kuronotokyo.com before trip to ask about allocation.

favorites.dial: deep green urushi lacquer
favorites.note: Same trio as Brown Cinnamon, green variant.

favorites.dial: vermilion urushi lacquer
favorites.note: Boldest of the trio. Red is brighter than your usual range.

favorites.dial: red-orange urushi, Hamon blade pattern
favorites.note: Very rare on secondary. Set search alerts.

favorites.dial: Hamon blade pattern dial
favorites.note: Hamon blade-pattern dial. Same case family as Urushi Aoyama.

favorites.dial: sunburst emerald green
favorites.note: Hits the muted-green sweet spot.

favorites.dial: Classic 37mm sector dial, brown
favorites.note: Classic-line sector dial in warm brown. Often the first piece for new collectors.

favorites.dial: eggshell white, time-only minimal
favorites.note: 50 pieces in eggshell. Two other colorways (Midnight Blue, Mystic Grey) also exist.

favorites.dial: burgundy 'azuki' bean tone, full calendar
favorites.note: 38mm pushes the upper bound but rich red-bean dial fits palette.

favorites.dial: warm orange sector
favorites.note: Smallest case in the Kurono family — perfect proportions for you.

favorites.dial: muted grey-green sector
favorites.note: Most affordable of the small-case favorites.

favorites.dial: akane red sector
favorites.note: From the 2023 34mm collection. Brighter red than your usual.

favorites.dial: Muonionalusta meteorite + white lacquer ring
favorites.note: Sold out Jan 15. Secondary listings appearing now. Best June-buy candidate.

favorites.dial: Comblémine, white or black, single-line minimal
favorites.note: 36.8mm, Comblémine dial, ~8 pcs/year total. Closed waitlist; secondary occasionally at A Collected Man.

favorites.dial: minimalist, sword-finish case
favorites.note: 7+ year waitlist, currently closed. Trunk-show or A Collected Man secondary only.

favorites.dial: Calatrava-inspired, ultra-minimal
favorites.note: Newest of the three. Even more restrained than the Ichimonji.

favorites.dial: small seconds, Breguet numerals, hand-engraved 10^60 motif
favorites.note: 38.6mm pushes the upper bound. Atelier-direct via lottery. Watchmaker is Walter Lange Award winner.

favorites.dial: Tsunami signature wave dial
favorites.note: Father atelier of Kurono. Tsunami is the accessible Asaoka piece (relatively).

favorites.dial: shibo urushi handcrafted
favorites.note: 40.6mm — over your sweet spot — but case finishing is exceptional.

favorites.dial: white pigment, art-deco striped center, Breguet numerals, snailed sub-seconds
favorites.note: Asaoka's white-dial evolution of the Tsunami in fully polished steel.

favorites.dial: Edo Kiriko hand-engraved sapphire with cherry blossom motif, rose-gold case
favorites.note: First wristwatch with Edo Kiriko hand-cut sapphire crystal — one-man Saitama atelier.

favorites.dial: grand feu enamel city ring, tantalum-tungsten case, art-deco worldtimer
favorites.note: Kyoto debut maker, GPHG 2024 nominee. First watch use of tantalum-tungsten alloy.

favorites.dial: German silver, urushi-filled hand-engraved Breguet numerals
favorites.note: Latest Type 1D iteration with screw caseback and refined hand-engraved Breguet numerals.

favorites.dial: DLC dark grey, diamond-shaped 3D hands, double-curved sapphire
favorites.note: 36mm sportier Type 4 in dark DLC grey — smallest in the NH lineup.

favorites.dial: salmon-pink frosted-leaves dial, triple calendar, black onyx cabochon crown
favorites.note: 60th Diamond Jubilee triple-calendar with salmon dial inspired by frost-column leaves.

favorites.dial: art-deco concentric day/night, lacquer-filled 24h bezel markers
favorites.note: Kurono's first world-time complication; bi-directional glazed 24h bezel.

favorites.dial: Yusai hand-finished textured dial, case-in-case Akita construction
favorites.note: Akita case-finishing flagship with hand-finished Yusai dial — under 400/yr total.

favorites.dial: silvered, hand-engraved, Breguet numerals, blued pear-shape hands
favorites.note: Dresden atelier; hand-engraved silver dial, Breguet hands. Pure Saxon classicism.

favorites.dial: silvered or enamel, hand-engraved guilloché, Roman numerals
favorites.note: Lang & Heyne's flagship — visible Saxon three-quarter plate, swan-neck regulator, gold chatons.

favorites.dial: vitreous grand-feu enamel, Paulin-designed numerals
favorites.note: The original anOrdain — Paulin Studio sans-serif numerals on grand-feu enamel.

favorites.dial: vitreous grand-feu enamel, fumé grey, Glasgow-painted
favorites.note: Glasgow workshop; hand-painted vitreous enamel fumé dials — the indie enamel reference at accessible tier.

favorites.dial: fumé (Funky Blue / Cosmic Green / Burgundy), no logo, no indices
favorites.note: The definitive logo-less fumé dial — Moser's purest minimalist statement.

favorites.dial: frosted silver, hand-engine-turned sub-second, blued hands
favorites.note: Norfolk-based; UT base hand-finished into a frost-and-guilloché dress watch.

favorites.dial: hand-engine-turned silver, Breguet numerals, small seconds
favorites.note: Lancaster-county made; in-house 801 caliber, rose-engine-turned dial. The American indie reference.

favorites.dial: fumé gradient with Sarpaneva moon-face aperture
favorites.note: Finnish indie; signature scalloped case and grimacing moon-face — distinctive but restrained.

favorites.dial: glossy black DLC, large tourbillon cage at 6
favorites.note: Asaoka's debut tourbillon — collab with OSG and Yuki Precision.

favorites.dial: Argentium silver, hand-engraved sub-dials
favorites.note: First NH perpetual calendar. 10 pieces total 2025-2026.

favorites.dial: lapis lazuli moon aperture, hand-engraved 18k yellow gold moon
favorites.note: Yellow-gold moonphase variant — lapis night sky and engraved gold moon. 5 pieces.

favorites.dial: 4-piece sterling Bombay, charcoal rubbing
favorites.note: MP1 in-house movement. May lottery — 10 pieces.

favorites.dial: sterling silver curved, green gold finish
favorites.note: Anonymous case (no logo). Companion to Sohkoku.

favorites.dial: two-part German silver sand-blasted, grand-feu enamel Morse code inlays
favorites.note: Norifumi Seki's debut: vertically-mounted hammer striking the dial as gong.

favorites.dial: semi-skeletonized rose gold, hand-finished tourbillon
favorites.note: Self-taught AHCI master's almost-entirely-hand-made tourbillon in 18k rose gold.

favorites.dial: opaline or grained, sub-seconds at 6, assegai hands
favorites.note: Ex-Patek master's restrained micro-rotor — assegai hands are the signature.

favorites.dial: silver guilloché or solid gold, twin barrels, sub-seconds + power reserve
favorites.note: Twin-barrel chronometer with 18k rose-gold movement.

favorites.dial: hand-guilloched solid gold/silver, multiple barleycorn/clous-de-Paris
favorites.note: First fully US-sourced watch in modern era.

favorites.dial: hand-guilloched silver/gold, hand-applied numerals
favorites.note: Finnish-Swiss hand-finishing benchmark. In-house movement with direct-impulse escapement.

favorites.dial: open-worked semi-skeletonized, large tourbillon cage at 6
favorites.note: Atelier tourbillon predecessor to Noir — polished steel with skeleton dial.

favorites.dial: traditional Japanese seasonal-hour wadokei, oxidised bronze
favorites.note: First Japanese AHCI member; world's only wristwatch wadokei converting traditional seasonal time.

favorites.dial: lacquered black, day/date sub-discs, applied indices
favorites.note: Original 2022 calendar; line is now retired so secondary only.

favorites.dial: bronze outer track with glossy black inner, no numerals
favorites.note: TiCTAC × Asaoka 36mm Reiwa edition limited to 50 pieces.

favorites.dial: midnight blue sunburst, polished sticks at 12/3/6/9
favorites.note: First-series 2019 Bunkyo Tokyo in deep blue.

favorites.dial: dark sunburst grey, no numerals, railroad minutes
favorites.note: Most under-the-radar of the original 2019 trio.

favorites.dial: silver-white with hand-designed Breguet numerals
favorites.note: First Kurono with Asaoka's bespoke Breguet numerals.

favorites.dial: deep black lacquer, Breguet numerals
favorites.note: Black-dial sibling of the Calligra trio.

favorites.dial: dark mother-of-pearl, Breguet numerals
favorites.note: MOP variant — most visually intricate of the Calligra dial trio.

favorites.dial: starburst guilloche-style center, polished sticks
favorites.note: 2025 successor to the Calligra special-project line.

favorites.dial: lapis-green metallic periphery with deep black mirrored center
favorites.note: Final Reiwa ever — only ~5% of dials passed QC. Line retired permanently.

favorites.dial: German silver, hand-engraved indices, sub-40 sport profile
favorites.note: Sub-40 sports-positioned variant in the Type 4 family.

favorites.dial: two-part German silver, 1930s-inspired rectangular
favorites.note: 26×43.5mm tonneau case, custom calibre 2524SS. 10 pieces 2024-2025.

favorites.dial: engraved German silver, Breguet numerals, sub-seconds
favorites.note: Smallest NH ever at 31mm with new Cal. 2326SS. 2026 release.

favorites.dial: Payne's Grey vitreous enamel fumé on silver, domed
favorites.note: Smoke-grey enamel fumé — collector favorite.

favorites.dial: deep oxblood vitreous enamel
favorites.note: Rich oxblood/burgundy enamel — saturated alternative to the Plum Fumé.

favorites.dial: deep solid Parisian-blue vitreous enamel
favorites.note: Solid (non-fumé) vitreous enamel blue with crisp gloss surface.

favorites.dial: warm caramel/tropical brown fumé enamel
favorites.note: Final fumé in the Model 2 series — 30 pieces, tropical-dial vibe.

favorites.dial: carved wood-grain pattern under aqua vitreous enamel
favorites.note: Method dials carve wood under translucent enamel — unique technique.

favorites.dial: enamel, Roman numerals, off-center sub-seconds
favorites.note: Classical small-seconds dress piece in the Saxon ruler-named series.

favorites.dial: enamel, Roman numerals, flying tourbillon at 6
favorites.note: House flagship tourbillon — atelier-flagship size exception at 41mm.

favorites.model: Endeavour Centre Seconds Concept Funky Blue (WG)
favorites.dial: Funky Blue fumé, no logo, no indices
favorites.note: WG version of the Funky Blue Concept — limited 100.

favorites.model: Endeavour Centre Seconds Concept Blue Lagoon (WG)
favorites.dial: Blue Lagoon fumé, tropical-water turquoise gradient
favorites.note: SIHH 2019 Blue Lagoon, 50pc WG.

favorites.model: Endeavour Centre Seconds Concept Cosmic Green
favorites.dial: Cosmic Green fumé, deep emerald-to-black gradient
favorites.note: Limited to 20 pieces — rarest of the Concept colors.

favorites.dial: Burgundy fumé, wine-red gradient
favorites.note: 5th-anniversary Concept color.

favorites.dial: engine-turned silver with applied chapter ring
favorites.note: Norwich-built Cal. DB-G06; 24-month build queue.

favorites.dial: openworked, hand-skeletonized bridges, applied indices
favorites.note: Updated S3 with openworked Calibre UT-G04.

favorites.dial: blue ribbed Viennese-modernism, alternating polished/brushed surfaces
favorites.note: Dial pattern echoes Adolf Loos / Josef Hoffmann ornament.

favorites.dial: ultra-thin checkerboard pattern in tribute to Josef Hoffmann
favorites.note: Architectural Hoffmann-grid dial — Vienna's clearest design statement.

favorites.dial: openworked hand-polished movement view
favorites.note: Skeleton variant exposing hand-finished Soprod-base movement.

favorites.dial: frosted silver, hand-engraved chapter ring, exposed tourbillon
favorites.note: Subscription tourbillon by France's most-watched young watchmaker.

favorites.dial: frosted gold, key-wound, libre excentrique escapement visible
favorites.note: Made entirely without CNC — every part hand-cut. Single-maker in Brittany.

favorites.dial: frosted gold, retrograde power-reserve hand at 12
favorites.note: Updated Eccentricity with retrograde PR; entirely hand-fabricated.

favorites.dial: industrial-modernist sandwich dial, sub-seconds
favorites.note: Designer-watchmaker pairing in Prague; private and limited editions.

favorites.dial: hand-carved freehand silver, hand-cut applied indices
favorites.note: Seoul's first internationally-recognized indie watchmaker.

favorites.dial: hand-finished silver, exposed tourbillon at 6
favorites.note: Han Moi Kim's atelier — Korea's quietest indie pioneer.

favorites.dial: lacquered with applied indices, made-to-order
favorites.note: Each watch assembled and fine-tuned in Stockholm.

favorites.dial: hand-forged Damascus steel pattern, blued steel hands
favorites.note: Master bladesmith + master watchmaker partnership; each Damascus dial unique.

favorites.dial: Damascus forged from Viking-style blade, raw or midnight blue
favorites.note: Dial forged from sword steel — literal Viking craft story.

favorites.dial: purist three-hand, hand-finished, in-house electroplating
favorites.note: Schramberg manufacture in old Junghans building.

favorites.dial: guilloché silver with jumping hour aperture, sub-minute disc
favorites.note: Svend Andersen's signature jumping hour at his 40th anniversary.

favorites.dial: red lacquer with hand-engraved rising-sun rays, jumping hour at 12
favorites.note: Japan-only platinum LE; rising-sun motif by Eric Ertz.

favorites.dial: hand-finished meteorite with mirror-polished moon
favorites.note: 39mm DB28; meteorite dial — most cosmic of the line.

favorites.dial: semi-skeletonized polished titanium, exposed balance
favorites.note: Ultra-thin variant with floating lug system and exposed balance.

favorites.dial: guilloché face-dial, hours/minutes as eyes, moonphase mouth
favorites.note: Most iconic Wristmon — multi-layer hand-finished face dial.

favorites.dial: silvered with Roman numerals, blued sword hands
favorites.note: The watch most non-collectors picture when they hear 'dress watch'.

favorites.dial: silver, baton indices, no date
favorites.note: The most recognized luxury watch silhouette in the world.

favorites.dial: silver opaline, applied indices, sub-seconds
favorites.note: Omega's restrained dress watch — closest the brand gets to gentleman territory.

favorites.dial: silver, applied baton indices, sub-seconds at 6
favorites.note: Patek's most restrained reference. The default 'serious dress watch' answer.

favorites.dial: silver-grained, applied indices, art-deco rectangular
favorites.note: Iconic 1931 reversible polo watch — the rectangular dress reference.

favorites.dial: white silver-plated, painted Arabic numerals, blued steel hands, Bauhaus
favorites.note: Quintessential small Bauhaus dress piece; hand-wound in-house Alpha caliber.

favorites.dial: solid silver, applied baton indices, two-hander
favorites.note: The most restrained Lange — 5.9mm-thin two-handed dress watch.

favorites.dial: embossed silver, Roman numerals
favorites.note: Tudor's classical dress watch — most accessible Rolex-family dress reference.

favorites.dial: matte black or sand, military Arabic numerals
favorites.note: Sub-$1K Swiss mechanical with WWII military DNA — the gateway watch.

favorites.dial: matte silver, painted numerals, domed plexi crystal
favorites.note: Pure 1961 Max Bill design language; hand-wound 34mm Bauhaus orthodoxy.

brands.name: Kurono Tokyo
brands.note: Sub-brand of Hajime Asaoka. Aoyama salon must-visit.
brands.bio: Hajime Asaoka's accessible line — atelier-grade design at sub-$3K, Miyota movements inside, Asaoka aesthetic outside. Drops sell out in minutes via timed online lottery. Salon Editions exist only in Tokyo.

brands.name: Naoya Hida & Co.
brands.style: Neo-classical, German silver dials, hand-engraved Romans, blued hands
brands.note: 75 watches/year. 25 to Armoury, 40 direct, 5 to Kamine Kobe.
brands.bio: 75 watches a year out of a Ningyocho atelier in Tokyo. German silver dials, hand-engraved Roman numerals, blued steel hands. Distribution: 25 to The Armoury, 40 direct, 5 to Kamine in Kobe.

brands.name: Hajime Asaoka
brands.style: Tsunami, Project T tourbillon — atelier flagship
brands.note: Parent atelier of Kurono. Tsunami in steel ~$30k.
brands.bio: Self-taught Tokyo watchmaker. Eight watches a year, often three of them tourbillons. The signature is the oversized balance wheel visible through the dial. AHCI candidate; widely considered Japan's most important contemporary independent.

brands.name: Kikuchi Nakagawa
brands.bio: Two former Citizen-trained watchmakers — Yusuke Kikuchi and ex-swordsmith Tomonari Nakagawa — making roughly eight watches a year in Tokyo. Lugs taper into a softly domed bezel; dials by Comblémine. Closed waitlist.

brands.name: Masa & Co (Masa's Pastime)
brands.style: Sterling silver Bombay dials, MP1 in-house
brands.note: Nagi & Sohkoku via lottery; Nayuta is Shinohara's apprentice line.

brands.name: Minase
brands.note: Divido is 40.6mm. 7 Windows at 38mm closer to fit.
brands.bio: Akita-prefecture tool-maker turned watchmaker. The whole brand turns on Sallaz polishing — leather-wheel hand finishing that produces distortion-free mirror surfaces on faceted cases. Divido and Horizon are the showcases.

brands.name: Daizoh Makihara
brands.bio: WOSTEP-trained Saitama watchmaker. Hand-built tourbillons and the world's first wristwatch use of Edo Kiriko hand-cut sapphire crystal. Some pieces include 3D petal-opening automata. Output: low single digits per year.

brands.name: Kazuo Maeda
brands.bio: Kyoto independent who emerged with one watch in 2024 — the Heures Universelles worldtimer — and immediately landed a GPHG nomination. Built around the first watchmaking use of tantalum-tungsten alloy. 30 pieces total.

brands.name: Vague Watch Co.
brands.bio: Japanese microbrand from 2010 — faux-patina, vintage-feel field and dress pieces under $1K. Industrial, not atelier. Included for stylistic context.

brands.name: Lang & Heyne
brands.style: Saxon classical — hand-engraved silver dials, S-shaped balance cocks
brands.bio: Dresden atelier in the Saxon tradition. S-shaped hand-engraved balance cocks, hand-cut wheels with pillared construction, German silver bridges. References named after Saxon kings: Friedrich August I, Konrad, Hektor. ~120 pieces a year.

brands.name: anOrdain
brands.bio: Glasgow workshop that revived British vitreous enamel dial production almost single-handedly. Sourced movements (Sellita), but the dial work and Paulin-designed typography are the whole point. Models 1 / 2 / 3 share a 38mm steel case.

brands.name: H. Moser & Cie.
brands.bio: Schaffhausen manufacture, fully vertically integrated (owns hairspring specialist Precision Engineering). The Endeavour Concept dials famously have no logo — Moser's purest minimalist statement. ~1,800 watches a year.

brands.name: Voutilainen
brands.bio: Kari Voutilainen's atelier in Môtiers, Switzerland — widely regarded as the finest movement finisher working today. The in-house Caliber 28 has a direct-impulse escapement; dials are made in-house at Comblémine. ~60 watches a year.

brands.name: Laurent Ferrier
brands.style: Galet plate cases, natural escapement, organic bridges
brands.bio: Geneva atelier founded by a 40-year Patek veteran. Galet 'plate' cases, natural escapement, organic-curve bridges that read more handcraft than Geneva geometry. ~225 pieces a year.

brands.name: F.P. Journe
brands.style: Rose gold movements, Invenit et Fecit
brands.bio: François-Paul Journe's Geneva manufacture, defined by 18k rose gold movements and the 'Invenit et Fecit' signature. The Chronomètre Souverain is the restrained pillar of the line. ~950 watches a year.

brands.name: Garrick
brands.bio: Norfolk-based attempt to revive English watchmaking at relative scale. Movements in collaboration with Andreas Strehler under the UT-G01 caliber. Frosted plates, hand-engraved balance cocks. Production in the low double digits.

brands.name: RGM
brands.style: American manufacture, in-house Cal. 801, rose-engine guilloché
brands.bio: Roland G. Murphy's Pennsylvania workshop — the leading American maker working in the traditional manufacture model. Caliber 801 was the first American-made wristwatch movement in decades. Murphy restores his own rose engines for the guilloché.

brands.name: J.N. Shapiro
brands.style: Hand-guilloché on restored rose engines, US-sourced movements
brands.bio: Joshua Shapiro's LA studio. The 2022 Resurgence is roughly 80-90% American-made — case, dial, many movement parts. Guilloché executed on antique rose engines Shapiro restored himself. Pieces commissioned directly.

brands.name: Sarpaneva
brands.bio: Stepan Sarpaneva's Helsinki brand — distinctive at a glance for the human-faced moonphase and the seven-armed scalloped case. Trained at Parmigiani / Vianney Halter. Output: low double digits to ~50 a year.

brands.name: Carl Suchy & Söhne
brands.bio: Austrian revival brand reborn in 2017, drawing on a 200-year-old Viennese watchmaking name. The Waltz N°1 dial pattern echoes Adolf Loos and Josef Hoffmann ornament — pure Wiener Werkstätte. Movements assembled in Switzerland, finished in Vienna.

brands.name: Théo Auffret
brands.bio: Young French independent who emerged with a hand-built tourbillon as his début piece — the Tourbillon à Paris souscription. Trained at Geneva's WOSTEP, now operates from Paris. Distributed exclusively via A Collected Man.

brands.name: Cyril Brivet-Naudot
brands.bio: Single-maker in coastal Brittany who builds his Eccentricity entirely without CNC — every wheel and pinion hand-cut on a treadle lathe. The 'libre excentrique' escapement is his own design. Roughly three watches a year.

brands.name: Prokop & Brož
brands.bio: Designer Tomáš Prokop and watchmaker Petr Brož, working from a Wenceslas Square atelier in Prague. Industrial-modernist sandwich dials. One of the few active Czech ateliers operating in the indie tradition.

brands.name: Minhoon Yoo
brands.bio: Korea's first internationally recognized indie watchmaker. Replaces guilloché with hand-carved freehand silver dials cut directly with a graver. The Carved Piece is the debut. Operating since the late 2010s.

brands.name: MOI Watch
brands.bio: Korea's most established indie watchmaker — design, production, and QC all by Han Moi Kim. Tourbillon-focused work with traditional hand-finishing. Korean horology's quiet pioneer.

brands.name: Sjöö Sandström
brands.bio: Swedish made-to-order watchmaker; each piece assembled and regulated in Stockholm. Royal Steel Classic 36mm is the brand's restrained dress reference. Smaller-batch than the Nordic peers.

brands.name: GoS Watches
brands.bio: Master bladesmith and master watchmaker partnership in Sweden, producing watches with hand-forged Damascus steel dials. The Fullerö Sword dial is forged from an actual Viking-style blade. Each Damascus dial is unique.

brands.name: Lehmann
brands.bio: German manufacture in the old Junghans building — Black Forest watchmaking with almost all parts produced in-house. The Intemporal 1 is the entry to the line. Uncommon among accessible-tier brands for actual vertical integration.

brands.name: Andersen Genève
brands.bio: Svend Andersen's Geneva atelier (founded 1980) — pioneering AHCI member known for jumping-hour displays and one-of-one pieces. Eric Ertz now leads the workshop. ~50 watches a year. The hidden gem of Geneva indie watchmaking.

brands.name: De Bethune
brands.style: Floating lugs, mirror-polished spheres, blued titanium
brands.bio: Founded 2002 by Denis Flageollet and David Zanetta — radical movement architecture (floating lugs, mirror-polished spheres, blued titanium). The DB28 series is the design statement. Around 200 watches a year.

brands.name: Konstantin Chaykin
brands.bio: Russian-born watchmaker who works on traditional and conceptual fronts — the Joker Wristmons reinterpret hours/minutes/moonphase as a face. AHCI member since 2010. Operates from Moscow.

brands.name: Cartier
brands.bio: Founded 1847 in Paris by Louis-François Cartier as a jeweler first. Louis Cartier invented the Tank in 1917, modeled on Renault tanks of WWI. Watch production has long since moved to Cartier's Swiss workshops in Le Locle. The Tank is the most recognizable dress-watch silhouette in the world.

brands.name: Rolex
brands.style: Oyster case, Perpetual rotor, mainstream luxury sport / dress
brands.bio: Founded 1905 in London by Hans Wilsdorf, relocated to Geneva in 1919. Pioneered the waterproof Oyster case in 1926 and the Perpetual rotor in 1931. Now the world's most recognized luxury watch maker, vertically integrated and entirely opaque.

brands.name: Omega
brands.style: Co-axial escapement, broad lineup spanning sport / dress
brands.bio: Founded 1848 in La Chaux-de-Fonds, Omega has been headquartered in Bienne since 1903. The Speedmaster went to the moon in 1969. Adopted George Daniels' co-axial escapement across the lineup post-2007 — the brand's most consequential horological move in decades.

brands.name: Patek Philippe
brands.bio: Founded 1839 in Geneva by Antoni Patek. Owned by the Stern family since 1932 — one of the last family-owned mainstream Swiss houses. The Calatrava 96, designed in 1932, defined the modern dress watch and remains the brand's restrained pillar reference.

brands.name: Jaeger-LeCoultre
brands.bio: Founded 1833 in Le Sentier by Antoine LeCoultre. Long known as the manufacture des manufactures — supplier of movements to many of Switzerland's most prestigious houses. The Reverso, born in 1931 as a polo solution with a flippable case, is the most recognized rectangular watch in horology.

brands.name: Tudor
brands.style: Rolex-sister at accessible tier, vintage-revival sport + dress
brands.bio: Founded 1946 in Geneva by Hans Wilsdorf as Rolex's accessible sibling. After decades of obscurity, the brand's vintage-revival relaunch in 2010 (Black Bay, Heritage Chronograph) restored Tudor as a serious mainstream alternative to its older sister.

brands.name: Hamilton
brands.bio: Founded 1892 in Lancaster, Pennsylvania. Made watches for American railroads and the US military through both World Wars. Now Swiss-owned by Swatch Group and produced in Bienne, but the design language and naming still draws on Hamilton's American military lineage.

brands.name: Junghans
brands.style: Bauhaus minimalism, Max Bill design lineage
brands.bio: Founded 1861 in Schramberg by Erhard Junghans. Was briefly the world's largest watchmaker before WWI. The 1961 collaboration with Bauhaus designer Max Bill produced the watches that still define the brand's identity — uncluttered painted dials, domed plexi crystals, hand-wound mechanical simplicity.

brands.name: NOMOS Glashütte
brands.style: Bauhaus dress, in-house Alpha and DUW calibers
brands.bio: Founded 1990 in Glashütte, immediately after German reunification, by Roland Schwertner. NOMOS produces ~25,000 watches per year — small by mainstream standards but large for an indie. The Tangente is the original Bauhaus reference; the in-house Alpha (hand-wound) and DUW caliber families now power the entire lineup.

brands.name: A. Lange & Söhne
brands.style: Saxon haute-horlogerie, three-quarter plate, untreated German silver
brands.bio: Founded 1845 in Glashütte by Ferdinand Adolph Lange — the founder of Saxon watchmaking. The brand was nationalized after WWII and disappeared until 1990, when Walter Lange (great-grandson) revived it after reunification. Now considered the German pinnacle: untreated German silver three-quarter plates, hand-engraved balance cocks, gold chatons. ~5,500 watches a year.

brands.name: Masahiro Kikuno
brands.bio: Tokyo-based AHCI full member since 2013, best known for the Wadokei reference — a modern wristwatch interpretation of Japan's traditional temporal hour system, where day and night hours vary in length with the seasons. Builds his movements almost entirely by hand, including wheels, escapements, and even hairsprings on certain pieces. Production is fewer than a handful of watches per year, with waiting lists extending several years.
